section.title: Pharaoh's Dreams
section.sectionRationale: Two years after the cupbearer forgets him, Pharaoh dreams two dreams no one in Egypt can read. Joseph, giving all glory to God, interprets them and proposes a plan to save the land. In a single day he is lifted from the dungeon to the second throne of Egypt, and the world comes to him for bread.
section.thesis: God raises His humbled servant from prison to the right hand of Pharaoh, giving him wisdom to preserve life through coming famine, so that Egypt and all nations are fed, a stunning display of providence that foreshadows the exalted Savior of the world.
section.frameNote: Six movements: Pharaoh's troubling dreams; the cupbearer's belated memory; Joseph before Pharaoh; the interpretation and rescue plan; Joseph exalted over Egypt; and the years of plenty, famine, and the nations coming for grain.

units.label: Pharaoh's troubling dreams
units.note: cows and grain, and no interpreter

verses.text: After two full years had passed, Pharaoh had a dream: He was standing beside the Nile,
when seven cows, sleek and well-fed, came up from the river and began to graze among the reeds.
After them, seven other cows, sickly and thin, came up from the Nile and stood beside the well-fed cows on the bank of the river.
And the cows that were sickly and thin devoured the seven sleek, well-fed cows. Then Pharaoh woke up,
but he fell back asleep and dreamed a second time: Seven heads of grain, plump and ripe, came up on one stalk.
After them, seven other heads of grain sprouted, thin and scorched by the east wind.
And the thin heads of grain swallowed up the seven plump, ripe ones. Then Pharaoh awoke and realized it was a dream.
In the morning his spirit was troubled, so he summoned all the magicians and wise men of Egypt. Pharaoh told them his dreams, but no one could interpret them for him.
Then the chief cupbearer said to Pharaoh, “Today I recall my failures.
Pharaoh was once angry with his servants, and he put me and the chief baker in the custody of the captain of the guard.
One night both the chief baker and I had dreams, and each dream had its own meaning.
Now a young Hebrew was there with us, a servant of the captain of the guard. We told him our dreams and he interpreted them for us individually.
And it happened to us just as he had interpreted: I was restored to my position, and the other man was hanged.”
So Pharaoh sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of the dungeon. After he had shaved and changed his clothes, he went in before Pharaoh.
Pharaoh said to Joseph, “I had a dream, and no one can interpret it. But I have heard it said of you that when you hear a dream you can interpret it.”
“I myself cannot do it,” Joseph replied, “but God will give Pharaoh a sound answer.”
Then Pharaoh said to Joseph: “In my dream I was standing on the bank of the Nile,
when seven cows, well-fed and sleek, came up from the river and began to graze among the reeds.
After them, seven other cows, sickly, ugly, and thin, came up. I have never seen such ugly cows in all the land of Egypt!
Then the thin, ugly cows devoured the seven well-fed cows that were there first.
When they had devoured them, however, no one could tell that they had done so; their appearance was as ugly as it had been before. Then I awoke.
In my dream I also saw seven heads of grain, plump and ripe, growing on a single stalk.
After them, seven other heads of grain sprouted, withered, thin, and scorched by the east wind.
And the thin heads of grain swallowed the seven plump ones. I told this dream to the magicians, but no one could explain it to me.”

units.label: The interpretation and the plan
units.note: seven years of plenty, seven of famine

verses.text: At this, Joseph said to Pharaoh, “The dreams of Pharaoh are one and the same. God has revealed to Pharaoh what He is about to do.
The seven good cows are seven years, and the seven ripe heads of grain are seven years. The dreams have the same meaning.
Moreover, the seven thin, ugly cows that came up after them are seven years, and so are the seven worthless heads of grain scorched by the east wind, they are seven years of famine.
It is just as I said to Pharaoh: God has shown Pharaoh what He is about to do.
Behold, seven years of great abundance are coming throughout the land of Egypt,
but seven years of famine will follow them. Then all the abundance in the land of Egypt will be forgotten, and the famine will devastate the land.
The abundance in the land will not be remembered, since the famine that follows it will be so severe.
Moreover, because the dream was given to Pharaoh in two versions, the matter has been decreed by God, and He will carry it out shortly.
Now, therefore, Pharaoh should look for a discerning and wise man and set him over the land of Egypt.
Let Pharaoh take action and appoint commissioners over the land to take a fifth of the harvest of Egypt during the seven years of abundance.
Under the authority of Pharaoh, let them collect all the excess food from these good years, that they may come and lay up the grain to be preserved as food in the cities.
This food will be a reserve for the land during the seven years of famine to come upon the land of Egypt. Then the country will not perish in the famine.”

units.label: Joseph exalted over Egypt
units.note: from the dungeon to the second throne

verses.text: This proposal pleased Pharaoh and all his officials.
So Pharaoh asked them, “Can we find anyone like this man, in whom the {{ruachelohim|Spirit of God}} abides?”
Then Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Since God has made all this known to you, there is no one as discerning and wise as you.
You shall be in charge of my house, and all my people are to obey your commands. Only with regard to the throne will I be greater than you.”
Pharaoh also told Joseph, “I hereby place you over all the land of Egypt.”
Then Pharaoh removed the signet ring from his finger, put it on Joseph’s finger, clothed him in garments of fine linen, and placed a gold chain around his neck.
He had Joseph ride in his second chariot, with men calling out before him, “Bow the knee!” So he placed him over all the land of Egypt.
And Pharaoh declared to Joseph, “I am Pharaoh, but without your permission, no one in all the land of Egypt shall lift his hand or foot.”
Pharaoh gave Joseph the name {{zaphenath|Zaphenath-paneah}}, and he gave him Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, to be his wife. And Joseph took charge of all the land of Egypt.
Now Joseph was thirty years old when he entered the service of Pharaoh king of Egypt. And Joseph left Pharaoh’s presence and traveled throughout the land of Egypt.
During the seven years of abundance, the land brought forth bountifully.
During those seven years, Joseph collected all the excess food in the land of Egypt and stored it in the cities. In every city he laid up the food from the fields around it.
So Joseph stored up grain in such abundance, like the sand of the sea, that he stopped keeping track of it; for it was beyond measure.
Before the years of famine arrived, two sons were born to Joseph by Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On.
Joseph named the firstborn {{manasseh|Manasseh}}, saying, “God has made me forget all my hardship and all my father’s household.”
And the second son he named {{ephraim|Ephraim}}, saying, “God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction.”
When the seven years of abundance in the land of Egypt came to an end,
the seven years of famine began, just as Joseph had said. And although there was famine in every country, there was food throughout the land of Egypt.
When extreme hunger came to all the land of Egypt and the people cried out to Pharaoh for food, he told all the Egyptians, “Go to Joseph and do whatever he tells you.”
When the famine had spread over all the land, Joseph opened up all the storehouses and sold grain to the Egyptians; for the famine was severe in the land of Egypt.
And every nation came to Joseph in Egypt to buy grain, because the famine was severe over all the earth.

ruachelohim.gloss: The Spirit of God. Even a pagan king recognizes that the source of Joseph's wisdom is divine, using the phrase first heard in Genesis 1:2. It marks Joseph as a Spirit-filled man of discernment, and points ahead to the promised Servant on whom the Spirit rests, Isaiah 11, fulfilled in Christ.

zaphenath.gloss: The Egyptian throne-name Pharaoh gives Joseph, probably meaning revealer of mysteries or God speaks and lives. It publicly seals Joseph's new status as savior of the land. The renaming shows a Hebrew slave now honored in Egypt, yet the narrative keeps naming God as the true source of the revelation.

manasseh.gloss: The name of Joseph's firstborn, sounding like the Hebrew for making to forget. Joseph confesses that God has made him forget his hardship and his father's house, not by erasing memory but by healing its sting. The name testifies that God turned decades of suffering into a place of peace.

ephraim.gloss: The name of Joseph's second son, sounding like the Hebrew for fruitful or doubly fruitful. Joseph confesses that God has made him fruitful in the very land of his affliction. The name declares that God brings abundance out of suffering, a pattern that reaches its height in the cross and resurrection of Christ.

basic.context: Two years after the cupbearer forgot him, Pharaoh has two dreams that no wise man in Egypt can interpret. The cupbearer finally remembers Joseph, who is rushed from prison. Giving credit to God, Joseph interprets the dreams as seven years of plenty followed by seven of famine, and proposes a plan. Pharaoh makes him ruler over all Egypt.
basic.hermeneutics: The key question is who deserves the credit for Joseph's rise. Joseph insists it is God who gives the answer and controls the future. The chapter is a display of God's providence lifting the humble and using one faithful man to save many lives.
basic.originalLanguages: Even Pharaoh sees that the Spirit of God is in Joseph. Joseph is given the Egyptian name Zaphenath-paneah, likely meaning revealer of mysteries. He names his sons Manasseh, God has made me forget my hardship, and Ephraim, God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction.
basic.typology: Joseph, lifted from prison to the throne, given a new name, and receiving the world that comes for bread, is a vivid picture of Christ, humbled then exalted, given the name above every name, and feeding all who come to Him. Every knee bowing before Joseph foreshadows every knee bowing to Jesus.
basic.christ: As Joseph was raised from the pit of prison to Pharaoh's right hand, so Christ was raised from death to the Father's right hand. Pharaoh's word, Go to Joseph and do whatever he tells you, echoes the call to come to Christ, the bread of life, for the salvation of all nations.
basic.god: God rules the whole scene. He sends the dreams, gives the interpretation, decrees the years of plenty and famine, and exalts Joseph at exactly the right moment. He is sovereign over kings and harvests, and He is working salvation for many through the suffering of one faithful servant.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 105 tells how God made Joseph lord of Pharaoh's house. Philippians 2 sings of Christ humbled and then exalted, before whom every knee will bow. John 6 presents Jesus as the bread of life for the hungry world.
basic.oneStory: This is the turning point of the Joseph story. God lifts His servant to power to preserve life through famine, keeping the covenant family alive and drawing the nations to Egypt for bread, a shining foreshadow of the exalted Christ who saves the world.

context.literary: This chapter is the great reversal of the Joseph narrative, answering the pit and the prison with the palace. It resolves the dream motif that began in Genesis 37, where Joseph's own dreams were mocked, and now dreams exalt him. The naming of his sons closes the unit with a theology of forgetting hardship and bearing fruit.
context.historical: Egypt's dependence on the Nile made it uniquely able to store grain against famine, and viziers who administered such reserves are known from Egyptian history. The signet ring, fine linen, gold chain, and second chariot are authentic marks of high Egyptian office. Marriage to a priest's daughter of On, that is Heliopolis, signals entry into the highest ranks.
context.canonical: The account grounds Israel's later sojourn in Egypt and the Exodus. Its portrait of a humbled sufferer exalted to save the nations becomes a template the New Testament applies to Christ in Philippians 2 and John 6, and Stephen recounts it in Acts 7 as part of the story of God's saving purpose.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Moses shows God sovereignly exalting Joseph to preserve life through famine, keeping the covenant family alive and blessing the nations through him, so that Israel would trust the God who rules kings, harvests, and history for the good of His people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's providential exaltation of Joseph; it is not a formula promising that faithfulness will always end in worldly power. Its enduring truths are God's sovereignty, the wisdom of preparing under God's guidance, and the pattern of suffering giving way to God-given fruitfulness.

debates.question: Do Joseph's Egyptian name, wife, and office represent worldly compromise or faithful service in a pagan land?

views.label: Faithful service without compromise
views.case: Joseph keeps confessing God before Pharaoh and names his sons in praise of God, showing that his heart remains loyal to the LORD even as he serves in Egypt's court; the trappings are the tools of his God-given task to save lives.

views.label: A note of danger to weigh
views.case: Some observe that a name honoring Egyptian religion and marriage into a priestly family could pull toward assimilation, and read the account as honest realism about serving in a pagan system, to be held alongside God's clear blessing on Joseph.

debates.bearing: Both agree Joseph's confession keeps God central. The passage encourages faithful engagement in secular spheres for the good of others, while alerting believers to guard their loyalty to God amid the honors of the world.

study.originalLanguages: Pharaoh himself testifies that the ruach elohim, the Spirit of God, dwells in Joseph, crediting a divine source for his wisdom. Joseph receives the Egyptian name Zaphenath-paneah, likely revealer of mysteries, a public seal on his role as savior of the land. And he names his sons in worship: Manasseh, from the word for making to forget, because God healed the sting of his hardship, and Ephraim, from the word for fruitful, because God made him fruitful in the land of his affliction. The two names together confess a God who both heals the past and brings abundance out of suffering.

typology.type: Joseph lifted from prison to the second throne
typology.fulfillment: Christ raised from death and exalted to the right hand of the Father.

typology.type: Every knee bowing before Joseph at Pharaoh's command
typology.fulfillment: Every knee bowing to the exalted Jesus, Philippians 2:9-11.

typology.type: Joseph opening the storehouses so the world may be fed
typology.fulfillment: Christ the bread of life, to whom all nations come to be saved from death.

study.christ: The chapter is a portrait of the humiliation and exaltation of Christ. Joseph is brought up out of the pit of prison, given a new name and the highest place, and set to rule as savior of the land. So Jesus, humbled to death, was raised and given the name above every name, seated at the Father's right hand. Pharaoh's decree that all should bow the knee anticipates the day when every knee bows to Christ, and the command, Go to Joseph and do whatever he tells you, echoes the invitation to come to Jesus, the true bread from heaven, so that the hungry of every nation may live. Joseph feeds Egypt and the world; Christ feeds all who come to Him with everlasting life.
study.god: God is the unseen sovereign of every scene: He sends Pharaoh's dreams, withholds their meaning from Egypt's wise men, prompts the cupbearer's memory at last, and reveals the future through Joseph, whom He alone equips. He decrees seven years of plenty and seven of famine and lifts His servant to the throne at the exact hour needed. Here is providence on a vast scale, ruling nations and harvests, turning long suffering into salvation, and keeping alive the family through whom the Redeemer of the world will come.

crossReferences.note: God summoned famine and sent Joseph ahead, making him lord of Pharaoh's house to instruct its rulers.
Christ humbled to death and exalted, given the name above every name, before whom every knee will bow.
Jesus is the bread of life; whoever comes to Him will never hunger, as the nations came to Joseph for bread.
Stephen recounts God giving Joseph favor and wisdom before Pharaoh and making him ruler over Egypt.

study.oneStory: This is the great reversal that turns the Joseph story toward salvation. God lifts His humbled servant to power precisely to preserve life through famine, keeping the covenant family alive and drawing all nations to Egypt for bread. In Joseph, raised from the pit, crowned, and feeding the world, we see cast in advance the exalted Christ, before whom every knee bows and to whom the hungry of every nation come to be fed with life that never ends.

quiz.q: How does Joseph answer Pharaoh's request to interpret his dreams?

options.text: He says he cannot do it himself, but God will give Pharaoh the answer.
options.note: Joseph gives all glory to God, verse 16.

options.text: He claims his own wisdom is greater than Egypt's magicians.
options.note: He credits God, not himself, though he is indeed wiser than the magicians.

options.text: He refuses to interpret until he is released.
options.note: He interprets freely and only later is exalted.

options.text: He guesses at a meaning to please Pharaoh.
options.note: He speaks God's true revelation, not flattery.

quiz.q: What do Pharaoh's two dreams mean, according to Joseph?

options.text: Seven years of great abundance followed by seven years of severe famine.
options.note: The cows and grain both signify the same fourteen years, verses 25 to 31.

options.text: Seven years of war followed by seven years of peace.
options.note: The dreams concern harvest and famine, not war and peace.

options.text: Fourteen years of unbroken prosperity.
options.note: The plenty is followed by famine, not continued prosperity.

options.text: Nothing; they were meaningless dreams.
options.note: God gave the dreams to reveal what He was about to do.

quiz.q: Why was Pharaoh's dream given in two forms, cows and grain?

options.text: Because the matter is firmly decreed by God and will happen soon.
options.note: The doubling signals certainty and imminence, verse 32.

options.text: Because Pharaoh misremembered a single dream twice.
options.note: They were two genuine dreams with one meaning, not a faulty memory.

options.text: Because the two dreams contradict each other.
options.note: They agree; they are one and the same message.

options.text: Because God was unsure of the outcome.
options.note: The doubling shows the outcome is fixed by God, not uncertain.

quiz.q: How is Joseph's exaltation in this chapter a picture of Christ?

options.text: Lifted from the pit to the throne, given a new name, with all bowing and coming to him for bread.
options.note: It mirrors Christ humbled then exalted, Philippians 2 and John 6.

options.text: He conquers Egypt in battle and enslaves its people.
options.note: Joseph rises by God's providence to save, not by conquest.

options.text: He rejects all power and remains in prison by choice.
options.note: God exalts him to rule and to save many lives.

options.text: He has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: The humiliation-then-exaltation pattern points clearly to Christ.

quiz.q: What do the names of Joseph's sons, Manasseh and Ephraim, confess?

options.text: That God healed the sting of his hardship and made him fruitful in the land of affliction.
options.note: The names praise God for forgetting pain and bearing fruit, verses 51 to 52.

options.text: That Joseph wanted to forget the God of his fathers.
options.note: He credits God for his healing; he has not abandoned the LORD.

options.text: That Joseph resented his suffering to the end.
options.note: The names testify to peace and fruitfulness, not lingering resentment.

options.text: That the names carry no meaning.
options.note: Both names are deliberate confessions of God's goodness.

quiz.q: What is the wider outcome of Joseph's rise by the end of the chapter?

options.text: The stored grain feeds Egypt and every nation that comes for bread during the famine.
options.note: All the earth comes to Joseph for grain, verse 57.

options.text: Egypt hoards its grain and lets the nations starve.
options.note: Joseph opens the storehouses and the nations come to buy, not starve unaided.

options.text: The famine never actually comes.
options.note: The famine comes just as Joseph said, testing and proving the plan.

options.text: Joseph loses his position when the famine begins.
options.note: He administers the whole rescue; his authority only grows.

questions: Joseph refused any credit and said God will give the answer. In your successes, how do you keep pointing others to God rather than to yourself?
God turned Joseph's long suffering into the means of saving many. How does Joseph's story help you trust that God can redeem your hardest years?
Joseph named his son Ephraim because God made him fruitful in the land of his affliction. Where might God be growing fruit in you through a painful place right now?
Pharaoh told the hungry, Go to Joseph and do whatever he tells you. What would it look like this week to go to Christ, the true bread, for the hunger only He can fill?
Every knee bowed before the exalted Joseph, foreshadowing every knee before Jesus. How does the certainty that Christ will be honored by all shape the way you live today?

translationNotes.note: In whom the Spirit of God abides can also be rendered the spirit of the gods; Pharaoh, a polytheist, nonetheless recognizes a divine source behind Joseph's wisdom.
Bow the knee renders an Egyptian word, Abrek, whose exact meaning is uncertain but which functioned as a call to show honor before Joseph.
Zaphenath-paneah is an Egyptian name of debated meaning, often given as revealer of mysteries or God speaks and lives; On is Heliopolis, a center of Egyptian sun worship.
